misconceptionSummary: Students use the wrong protractor scale when drawing, producing 180° minus the intended angle.

lines.text: Student draws angle that looks clearly obtuse  ✗
40° is acute — the wrong scale gave 140°

steps.narration: The task is to draw forty degrees — an acute angle. The student's drawing looks obtuse. That is the sign of a wrong-scale error: reading forty from the wrong scale gives one hundred and forty, which is obtuse.

visuals.ruleText: The correct scale starts at 0° where the baseline arm sits — count from there
visuals.subText: For an acute angle like 40°, the mark must be in the acute region — not past 90°

steps.narration: Before marking, identify which scale reads zero at the baseline. Forty degrees on that scale will be in the acute region — between zero and ninety. If the mark would fall past ninety, you have the wrong scale.

lines.text: Baseline along arm, centre on vertex
Find 40° on scale starting at 0° on the baseline
Mark, remove, join — angle is acute  ✓

steps.narration: With the baseline correctly aligned, find forty on the scale that starts at zero there. Make a dot at forty, remove the protractor, and draw a line from the vertex to the dot. The angle should look clearly acute — narrower than a right angle.

visuals.expression: 40° drawn correctly — acute, narrower than a right angle
visuals.label: Use the scale starting at 0° on your baseline — sanity-check the angle type

steps.narration: Forty degrees — a correct acute angle. Use the scale starting at zero on the baseline, and always check the finished drawing looks like the intended angle type.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: drawing 140° instead of 40° (read the wrong scale). The correct scale starts at 0° on the baseline arm. A 40° angle is acute — the drawn angle must look narrower than a right angle.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: When drawing an angle, use the scale that starts at 0° on your baseline. If the result looks like the wrong type — obtuse when you wanted acute — you used the other scale. Subtract from 180° to see what you drew.
